data.title: B. Riley Financial, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
B. Riley Financial, Inc., a diversified financial services platform, has released its annual 10-K report, detailing the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's activities and future outlook, reflecting its commitment to reducing indebtedness and enhancing operational efficiency.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenues: $838.6 million, a decrease of $627.2 million from the previous year, primarily due to a decrease in fair value adjustments on loans, interest income from securities lending, and higher trading losses.

Operating (Loss) Income: $(475.7) million, a significant decline from $96.1 million in the previous year, driven by decreased revenues and increased impairment charges.

Net Loss: $(774.9) million, compared to a net loss of $(105.6) million in the previous year, largely due to decreased operating income and increased realized and unrealized losses on investments.

Net Loss Available to Common Shareholders: $(772.3) million, reflecting a substantial increase in net loss compared to $(108.0) million in the prior year, influenced by lower revenues and higher impairment charges.

Business Highlights

Business Overview: B. Riley Financial, Inc. operates as a diversified financial services platform offering investment banking, brokerage, wealth management, asset management, direct lending, and business advisory services. The company serves a broad client base, including public and private companies, financial sponsors, investors, and individuals.

Operational Focus: In 2024 and 2025, the company has prioritized reducing indebtedness through strategic asset dispositions and monetizations, successfully decreasing total outstanding debt from $2.4 billion to $1.8 billion.

Geographical Presence: B. Riley is headquartered in Los Angeles, California, with offices across the U.S. and internationally in Canada, Europe, Asia, and Australia, supporting its global operations.

Capital Markets Segment: This segment provides investment banking and institutional brokerage services, fund and asset management, and direct lending services. The company maintains an investment portfolio of public and private equities and debt securities.

Wealth Management Segment: The segment offers retail brokerage, investment management, and insurance services. As of December 31, 2024, assets under management totaled approximately $20.7 billion, with a significant portion sold to Stifel Financial Corp. in 2025.

Financial Consulting Segment: The company provides specialized advisory services, including bankruptcy, restructuring, and forensic accounting. In 2025, B. Riley sold its GlassRatner and Farber advisory businesses.

Communications Segment: This segment includes businesses like Lingo Management and magicJack, focusing on cloud communications and VoIP services. The company may pursue future acquisitions to expand this portfolio.

Consumer Products Segment: Comprising Tiger US Holdings, Inc. (Targus), this segment designs and sells consumer and enterprise productivity products globally, including laptop cases and docking stations.

E-Commerce Segment: Nogin, Inc. operates e-commerce stores providing Commerce-as-a-Service solutions. In 2025, the company transferred Nogin's assets to an assignee for creditor benefit, ceasing its control over these operations.

Future Outlook: B. Riley anticipates continued focus on debt reduction and strategic asset monetization. The company remains committed to leveraging its diversified platform to meet client needs and drive operational improvements.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: B. Riley Financial, Inc. has been actively focusing on strategic asset dispositions and monetizations to reduce its total indebtedness, which decreased from $2.4 billion at December 31, 2023, to $1.8 billion at December 31, 2024. The company has also engaged in several transactions, including the sale of its Great American Group and the divestiture of its Lingo/Bullseye carrier business, to streamline operations and focus on core business segments. Additionally, the company has been involved in private exchange transactions to manage its debt profile, exchanging existing senior notes for new 8.00% Senior Secured Second Lien Notes due 2028.

Capital Management: The company has undertaken significant capital management activities, including the redemption of all issued and outstanding 6.375% 2025 Notes and the completion of a new credit agreement with Oaktree Capital Management, L.P., which provided a $125.0 million secured term loan facility. B. Riley also issued warrants to Oaktree affiliates to purchase shares of its common stock, enhancing its capital structure. Furthermore, the company completed a private exchange transaction to manage its senior notes, reducing interest expenses and extending maturities. The company has also temporarily suspended dividends on its Series A and B Preferred Stock to preserve cash flow.

Future Outlook: B. Riley Financial, Inc. anticipates that the reduction of indebtedness will remain a key priority for the foreseeable future, potentially through additional asset dispositions or monetization transactions. The company plans to continue focusing on strategic initiatives that enhance liquidity and operational efficiency, while exploring opportunities for capital investment in its core business segments. The company is also committed to managing its debt profile through strategic exchanges and refinancing activities to optimize its capital structure.

Challenges and Risks

Market Risks: The company's revenues and results of operations are volatile and difficult to predict, impacted by recent divestiture transactions and changes in trade policy and regulations, including tariffs and retaliatory measures. Market volatility could lead to a decline in transaction volumes, affecting revenue from commissions and spreads.

Operational Risks: The company is exposed to significant legal liability, which could lead to substantial damages. Ineffective risk management processes and strategies may result in losses. The company also faces risks related to information technology disruptions, data security breaches, and unauthorized disclosures of sensitive data.

Regulatory Risks: The company is subject to net capital and other regulatory capital requirements. Failure to comply with these rules could significantly harm the business. Increased regulation in the financial services industry could increase costs and limit business opportunities.

Emerging Risks: Climate change presents both immediate and long-term risks, potentially impacting operations, investments, and client financial conditions. The transition to a less carbon-dependent economy may also negatively impact the company and its clients.

Credit Risks: The company is exposed to credit risk from various activities, including loans and guarantees. The inability to fully realize the value of collateral securing loans could result in significant losses.

Reputational Risks: Recent events related to investments in Freedom VCM and prior business relationships have adversely affected the company's reputation, operations, and stock price. Ongoing legal proceedings and unfounded allegations by short sellers continue to pose risks.

Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by market volatility and regulatory changes. Strategies to address these include enhancing risk management processes, improving internal controls, and diversifying revenue streams to mitigate the impact of market fluctuations.

Market Risk Management: The company is exposed to fluctuations in currency exchange rates, which could adversely affect financial results. Management is focused on monitoring these risks and implementing hedging strategies where appropriate to mitigate potential adverse impacts.

SEC Filing: B. Riley Financial, Inc. [ RILY ] - 10-K - Sep. 19, 2025

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1558 ET - Big Tech companies are among the top employers of highly skilled foreign workers that use the H-1B visa program, which President Trump is now targeting with a new $100,000 fee per application. Amazon.com is the biggest user of the program, with more than 10,000 beneficiaries approved, according to federal data. Microsoft and Meta Platforms are among the top users as well, with more than 5,000 program beneficiaries each, the data shows. Behind them are Apple and Alphabet's Google with more than 4,000 beneficiaries apiece. The move comes two weeks after tech giant CEOs--including Google's Sundar Pichai, Apple's TIm Cook and Meta's Mark Zuckerberg--had dinner at the White House and took turns praising Trump. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1509 ET - The first days following the iPhone 17 launch have shown better-than-expected demand, say JPMorgan analysts, who now expect higher profits for Apple in the coming years. Despite concerns that iPhone 16's release last year would lead to a pull-forward in demand that would hurt iPhone 17 sales, early indications show sales volumes could be better than iPhone 16's sales. "We expect the momentum to accelerate into FY26 with the launch of the foldable iPhone setting up for a stronger upgrade cycle in FY27 along with acceleration in pricing tailwinds, leading to our forecast of low double-digit iPhone revenue growth in FY27," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1448 ET - After 2023's "Taylor Swift: The Eras Tour" concert film collected more than $261 million globally, it's no surprise that AMC Theatres Distribution is again collaborating with Swift. "Taylor Swift: The Official Release Party of a Showgirl" will play at all 540 U.S. AMC locations. It will also be at the Cinemark and Regal chains in the U.S. and will be available in over 100 countries. Unlike the previous blockbuster movie, this is a limited run slated for Oct. 3 through Oct. 5 in the U.S. AMC notes that "while normally prohibited during traditional showtimes, AMC guests are welcome to sing and dance" during the movie, but may not stand on seats or block aisles or stairs. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

1422 ET - Investors will get a chance to grill Constellation Software about the company's thoughts on AI. The Canadian software provider, one of the biggest companies in Canada by market cap, on Monday will convene a Q&A session featuring four of its AI specialists, with the goal of shedding some light on how the emerging technology will impact its business and the industry. In announcing the event earlier this week, Constellation President Mark Leonard noted that the idea was spawned by an inquisitive shareholder, and the company instead suggested the webcast available to a wider audience. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1402 ET - Intuit sounded increasingly confident at its latest investor day as it looks to accelerate growth toward 20%, Susquehanna analysts James Friedman and Spencer Anson say in a note. A few of the company's previous big bets seem to be paying off, including its shift toward mid-market businesses and furthering its money management offerings for small businesses, the analysts say. Intuit's artificial intelligence strategy is also noteworthy, with the company deploying agents and automation tools across its platform to cut down on hours of administrative work for consumers and businesses, they say. Shares tick up 2.2%, to $689.82. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Bill Holdings is due for a bull run as the company tries to outperform low Wall Street growth forecasts, Truist analysts say in a research note as they upgrade shares to buy from hold. The business-assistance software company is set up to exceed analyst expectations in core revenue growth with both easier comps ahead and better-than-expected customer growth in its Embedded Solutions segment, the analysts say. Bill stock has been a laggard in the fintech sector for the past couple of years due to both a lack of investor interest in small- to mid-cap fintech companies and a slowdown in core revenue growth, but that has reset the stock low enough for investors to become bullish again, the analysts say. They also see sizable long positions held by activist shareholders as a plus. Shares rise 0.6% to $52.90. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1153 ET - BlackBerry should be able to achieve or beat its guidance in its fiscal 2Q, CIBC's Todd Coupland says in a report. He says BlackBerry's average surprise compared with FactSet is 7% for revenue and almost triple for adjusted EBITDA since John Giamatteo took the helm as CEO at the end of 2023. "We expect this encouraging trend to continue," he says, noting thatshow progress in secure communications, its secure messaging and cybersecurity platform for governments and enterprises, as well as QNX, which is an embedded operating system that is used in automotive, medical and industrial applications. "We see these trends accelerating throughout F2026," Coupland adds. BlackBerry reports its 2Q on Sept. 25. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0826 ET - Intel's deal with Nvidia adds much needed funding for its foundry strategy, analysts say. By working with Intel, Nvidia is supporting the only viable leading-edge domestic foundry, D.A. Davidson analysts says. Intel can shore up its foundry capacity via packaging for these products, they say. Nvidia's $5 billion investment in Intel follows the U.S. government's 10% stake in the company, which equates to about $9 billion, according to Oppenheimer analysts. "INTC benefits from this deal through additional investment to support the company's capex intensive foundry strategy," the Oppenheimer analysts say. SoftBank also said back in August it was planning to purchase $2 billion worth of Intel shares. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0759 ET - Intel's vote of confidence from Nvidia this week is a tipping point for the company, Benchmark analyst Cody Acree says, upgrading the stock to buy from hold. Enthusiasm in Intel's x86 roadmap is much needed as it's been losing share to AMD and others, Acree says. Alongside the $5 billion equity investment from Nvidia, the partnership will likely make Intel more competitive in data center server sockets and pull it into more data center deals. Acree also expects Intel to get additional investments from other companies, as the Nvidia deal was said to have been the culmination of months of discussions. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0447 ET - ASML Holding is trading attractively thanks to better newsflow in recent weeks, Citi analysts write in a research note. Investors were disappointed after the Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment said in July that it could no longer guarantee growth in 2026 amid uncertainty from President Trump's tariffs. Now, the European Union has a deal with the U.S. setting a 15% tariff ceiling for semiconductor exports. Meanwhile, news at Intel, a key ASML client, has improved. The U.S. government is taking a 10% stake in Intel, and Nvidia said it would inject $5 billion into Intel as part of a collaboration to develop custom data-center and personal-computing products. ASML shares trade 0.2% lower at 792.20 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0135 ET - China's biggest bottleneck in reducing reliance on Nvidia chips is its limited chip manufacturing capability due to U.S. restrictions, say Jefferies analysts in a research note. A flurry of new local AI chips announcements recently have boosted market confidence in China's chip self-reliance. However, China's most advanced contract chip maker, SMIC, still faces constraints on producing 7-nanometer chips without advanced equipment, they say. Huawei's plan last year to roll out Ascend 910D using 5-nanometer technology hasn't materialized due to poor yield, the analysts say. Should U.S. and China reach a trade deal, there could be some potential easing of U.S. export restrictions on chip-making equipment, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0032 ET - Nvidia's $5 billion investment puts Intel back at the center of the AI ecosystem, Saxo chief investment strategist Charu Chanana says in a research note. For Intel to co-develop CPUs that plug directly into Nvidia's GPUs, its chips move from a supporting role to a core driver of performance, the strategist notes. Meanwhile, the deal gives Intel fresh credibility with customers and "plants seeds for Nvidia to eventually use Intel's foundry as a second manufacturing source if TSMC capacity becomes strained or geopolitics intervene," she adds. For Nvidia, the partnership provides "flexibility and reach," as it strengthens Nvidia's grip on AI data centers and opens the door to new categories of AI PCs, she says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0319 ET - Medikaloka Hermina is poised to benefit from Astra International's doubling of its stake to 20%, Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia analysts say in a research report. This transaction supports the hospital-chain operator's long-term growth potential in Indonesia's private hospital sector and strengthens its alignment with a leading Indonesian conglomerate, the analysts say. Astra International's increased involvement is likely a mutually beneficial development. Synergies between the two companies and the success of Medikaloka Hermina's strategy to target the mid- to high-end market should serve as additional upside to its growth. The stock remains one of the brokerage's top picks in the sector, with a buy rating and a target price of IDR1,800.00. Shares are 0.6% lower at IDR1,675.00.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2357 ET - ResMed's bull at Citi thinks the market is underestimating the breath-tech provider's medium-term margin-expansion capacity. Analyst Laura Sutcliffe points out that consensus for a fiscal 2026 gross margin of 62% is in-line with guidance, but ignores management's later growth ambitions. She thinks that 63% is achievable in fiscal 2027 and beyond. A good performance through fiscal 2026 could prompt analysts to rerate the stock, she writes in a note. Citi initiates coverage of ResMed's U.S.-listed stock with a US$330.00 target price and a buy rating. It also rated ResMed's Australia-listed stock a buy, and lifts its target price 4.1% to A$51.00. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2319 ET - Cochlear loses its bull at Citi, where analyst Laura Sutcliffe sees potential for affordability issues to weigh on consumer demand for its hearing tech. Sutcliffe tells clients in a note that she remains optimistic that the Australia-listed company can maintain its market-leading position, but suspects that fiscal 2026 growth will be weighted toward the second half of the period. She points out that while the knowledge that newer products were on the way meant some patients were reluctant to upgrade in fiscal 2025, affordability was also a factor in delayed spending. Citi cuts its target price by 8.6% to A$320.00 and reduces its recommendation to neutral from buy. Shares are down 1.0% at A$292.00. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2137 ET - Hartalega's earnings outlook appears stable even as uncertainties over the supply and demand of gloves linger heading into 2026, Hong Leong Investment Bank analyst Chee Kok Siang says in a note. The glove maker is decommissioning its less efficient Bestari Jaya facilities while upgrading production lines and automation capabilities, he says. These moves are expected to reinforce its position as Malaysia's most efficient producer of generic medical examination gloves, he notes. Hartalega's strong net cash of 986 million ringgit as of 1Q provides financial flexibility to weather industry challenges, he adds. Hong Leong maintains a hold rating on Hartalega and keeps its target price at MYR1.32. Shares are 0.8% lower at MYR1.19. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com) (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2100 ET - Pharmaceutical supplier Ebos shakes its bear at Citi following the recent sharp selloff in its stock. With the stock down by more than 25% since the dual-listed company reported its annual results on Aug. 27, the investment bank raises its recommendation to neutral from sell. Analyst Laura Sutcliffe says shares are now trading at about 17 times estimated FY 2027 core earnings, which is close to their historical average. She sets her target price a little lower to reflect the softer-than-expected margins reported last month. Citi cuts its target price on Ebos's Australia-listed stock by 12.5% to A$28.00. Shares are up 3.0% at A$26.15. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1942 ET - There doesn't appear to be a quick fix for Ramsay Health Care's Elysium mental health services business in the U.K., according to Citi. "Persistent occupancy problems and cost pressures lead us to think the road to a better performance is at least two years long," analyst Laura Sutcliffe says. Elysium's underlying Ebit fell 61% in FY 2025, compared to a 17% rise by its other U.K. operations. Earlier this year Ramsay paused investment in adding new sites for Elysium as it concentrates on improving performance. For Citi, Ramsay is effectively in damage limitation mode. "We do not rule out more drastic action from management," the bank says. It has a neutral call on Ramsay, and trims its price target by 10% to A$36.00/share. Ramsay ended Thursday at A$32.95. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1455 ET - Lennar is backing-off its strategy of maintaining high sales volume through a dormant housing market at the expense of margins, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. Lennar's F3Q sales incentives rose to 14.3%, reducing margin by almost five percentage points. But Miller said on an analyst call that Lennar is now lowering delivery expectations for F4Q and the full year, "to relieve the pressure on sales and deliveries and help establish a floor on margin." Miller says Lennar is recalibrating "to make sure that we're not pushing too hard on a market that really doesn't want to be pushed." Lennar is off 3.7% at $127.99. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1347 ET - Mortgage rates are steadily declining, falling this week to their lowest level since 2024. But that yet hasn't led to a boost in sales for Lennar, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "Even though mortgage rates began to trend downward towards the end of the quarter, stronger sales have not yet followed," Miller says. But the company is starting to see stronger customer interest and traffic and expects sales to eventually pick up if mortgage rates continue to fall. "We are optimistic that if mortgage rates approach the 6% level or even lower, we will soon see some firming in the market," Miller says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0407 ET - Asian economies and assets are likely to benefit from the Fed's decision to ease earlier this week, say economists and strategists at Manulife Investment Management. The Fed's move is expected to bring positive momentum for emerging markets, while the resumption of its easing cycle could loosen financial conditions, boost investor sentiment, which bodes well for risk assets. Asia can attract continued capital inflows as the Fed's cut supports risk appetite. "With lower inflationary pressures and a weaker U.S. dollar, EM Asia currencies have the scope to catch up in an environment where volatility remains low, especially for high yielders in the region," they say.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0106 ET - The Bank of Japan's planned sale of domestic equity exchange-traded funds may be insignificant to the market in terms of its pace of selling. The BOJ says it intends to sell ETFs worth about 620 billion yen in market value annually, which is the same pace it had been selling its domestic stock holdings until July this year. Despite the BOJ's stock sales and policy-rate increases, the Japanese stock market has maintained its rising trend in recent years to hit record highs. The central bank purchased domestic stocks from banks in the early 2000s and in the wake of the 2008 global financial crisis as part of extraordinary measures to support the financial system. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1911 ET [Dow Jones]--Morgan Stanley estimates ANZ needs to find up to A$2.0 billion of cost savings if it wants to achieve likely medium-term targets under new CEO Nuno Matos. Analyst Richard E. Wiles thinks twin goals of 11-12% return on equity and a CTI ratio of less than 48% within three years would be plausible. MS says its forecasts already assume cost savings totaling A$1.15 billion, including from changes to simplify the bank. "We estimate that even greater savings of A$1.5 billion-A$2.0 billion would require cost reduction of 15-20% in Australian banking and the group center, with a reduction of 10.0-12.5% in the Institutional and New Zealand divisions," MS says. Its price target rises 2.7% to A$30.50/share. ANZ ended Thursday at A$32.86. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1521 ET - Crude futures fall for a third straight session to end the week little changed with the market shrugging off an additional round of proposed EU sanctions against Russia. They include actions against vessels carrying Russian oil and buyers of Russian oil. The three days of declines wiped out gains made early in the week on Ukrainian strikes against Russian oil facilities. "The balancing act of OPEC+ oversupplying the global market against a possible drop in Russian oil sales is keeping crude futures in very tight trade," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. WTI settles down 1.4% at $62.68 a barrel, off 1 cent from a week ago. Brent falls 1.1% to $66.68 a barrel and is 0.5% lower on the week.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1353 ET - Royal Bank of Canada expects data to show the Canadian economy rebounded with vigor in July following a dismal 2Q. The bank's economists tell clients they are penciling in a 0.2% month-over-month increase in industry-level GDP, when Statistics Canada issues the monthly data next week. The data agency has forecast a 0.1% jump. RBC points to solid volume gains in exports, manufacturing shipments and wholesale transactions, and an anticipated pickup in oil production in western Canada after forest fires forced companies to curtail production. Employment fell sharply in July, but RBC notes that the drop in hours worked was not as steep. Overall, RBC expects "modest" GDP growth in 3Q after a 1.6% annualized drop in 2Q. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1332 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose by two this week to 418 in a fourth straight increase, and was 70 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. With a potential drop in oil prices below $60 this winter on oversupply concerns, some shale producers might struggle to break even "and could be forced to shut in significant volumes of production, potentially rebalancing the market and pushing prices back into the $60s," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "Rig counts in the U.S. have been steadily declining since 2023, with an accelerated drop since early this year, indicating that producers are already scaling back growth and may soon begin curtailing output." Natural gas rigs are steady at 118, up by 22 from a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1046 ET - Canadian electricity flows into the Northeastern U.S. have been declining in recent years, partly because of drought limiting Canada's hydroelectricity generation and partly because of lower demand in the New York and New England independent system operators, the EIA says in a note. Canadian electricity imports provided 11% of NY-ISO electricity between 2016 and 2022. That fell to 3% in 2024 and 2% through August of this year. Canadian imports play a key role at times of high demand. In January Canada supplied 14% of NE-ISO's power, although on average through August it provided 5% compared with 14% between 2016 and 2022. While typically importers, "shifting trends have led to multiple days of net electricity exports into Canada from the two ISOs," the EIA adds. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1007 ET - Citi analysts keep a "less-bearish-than-consensus" view of oil prices, seeing Brent easing toward $60 a barrel by the end of this year and into 2026. The OPEC+ decision to start unwinding a second layer of output cuts adds to expected global stock builds, which Citi now pegs at 1.1 million barrels a day in 2025 and 2.1 million b/d in 2026. The bank's bear case that could push Brent toward $50 could come about if global demand erodes on weaker macroeconomics and trade, supply rises faster than expected, "and if China stockpiling disappoints," they say in their quarterly outlook. The low-probability bull case with Brent above $75 would be driven by geopolitical events such as attacks on Russian infrastructure or an Israel-Iran escalation. Brent is off 0.7% at $66.97 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0915 ET - Oil futures are lower as the EU unveils a new package of sanctions against Russia that targets banks, oil vessels and buyers of Russian oil, while lowering the crude oil price cap. Robert Yawger of Mizuho said this week that the previous 18 rounds of EU sanctions since the 2022 invasion of Ukraine "have proven largely ineffective in stopping the flow of Russian oil exports." Ukrainian attacks on Russian oil facilities lifted prices early this week. WTI is down 0.5% at $63.26 a barrel and Brent is off 0.4% at $67.20. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0212 ET - Electricity Generating's acquisition of 30% stake in Indonesia's Chandra Daya Investasi unlocks value, CGS International's Rasmiman Sermprasert says in a research report. This stake is expected to contribute at least 11% of the Thai company's total profit per year over 2026-2027, the analyst estimates. Hence, the brokerage lifts its 2026 and 2027 core net profit forecasts for Electricity Generating by 57% and 37%, respectively, reflecting the earnings accretion from the Indonesian company, which is now a publicly listed entity in Indonesia. The brokerage raises the stock's rating to add from hold and the target price to THB130.00 from THB110.00. Shares are 0.4% higher at THB116.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1922 ET [Dow Jones]--Optimists on Santos will pivot to a "break up" argument in the aftermath of the XRG-led consortium dropping its $18.7 billion takeover offer, Morgans says. For those investors, the sum of Santos's parts are worth more than its whole and separate domestic natural-gas and LNG businesses would support higher valuations. "Certainly possible, but given the reserve constraints faced by both GLNG and Darwin LNG we do not treat this as a probable scenario to consider," analyst Adrian Prendergast says, referring to Santos's LNG facilities in Australia. Morgans cuts Santos to trim, from accumulate, saying the stock looks modestly overvalued on fundamentals alone. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1858 ET [Dow Jones]--Santos's share price is likely to remain volatile in the near term as investors adjust to the Australian energy company no longer being a bid target of the XRG consortium, Morgan Stanley says. Investors in Santos must digest several uncertainties, including around domestic energy supply and the decommissioning of some assets. It will take time for fundamentals, such as Santos's free cash flow yield, to reassert themselves, analyst Rob Koh says. MS cuts its price target on Santos by 21% to A$7.00/share, but retains an equal-weight call. Karoon Energy presents the most upside for investors among Australian energy stocks, MS says. Santos ended Thursday at A$6.74, down 12% across that session after the XRG consortium withdraw its bid proposal. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1422 ET - Front-month gold futures finish the week higher as investors look toward a potential rate cut next month. "Markets are pricing in a 92% chance of a rate cut from the Fed in October, following Wednesday's cut that sent mixed signals across markets," says ADM Investor Services in a note. This is supportive for gold, says ADMIS, calling Federal Reserve policy "the key driver in price direction." What's also supporting gold is the continued appetite from central banks to build up gold inventories, says the firm. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1414 ET - Nations around the world are considering stepping up trade protection measures on the heels of a unified tariff on all U.S. steel imports, Fitch Ratings says. Several countries are considering increasing domestic production, as well as anti-dumping and safeguard measures in less-protected markets such as India, Turkey and Brazil, according to Fitch. The EU could see the introduction of a "melted and poured" rule, Fitch adds. That rule is a trade measure which determines the origin of steel and metal goods by the location of initial melting and casting. "We expect trade flows to shift, with high-cost producers becoming unable to compete with lower-cost steelmakers," Fitch says, adding this could include changes in U.S. import patterns, with higher-cost steel possibly being diverted from the U.S. into less-protected markets. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

0401 ET - London-listed miners gain in early morning European trading as gold ticks higher. Gold futures are up 0.2% at $3,685.20 a troy ounce and nearly 10% higher this month on U.S. interest rate cut hopes and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop. Endeavour Mining and Fresnillo are leading the FTSE 100 index risers, up 3% and 2.5%, respectively. Anglo American shares are up 1.3%, while Antofagasta shares are trading 1% higher (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0238 ET - Comex gold futures have likely shifted into a phase of consolidation, based on the daily chart, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a report. The futures' bearish price action on Thursday confirms strong resistance has formed at $3,700/oz, the analyst says. The commodity's bullish momentum has decelerated, as indicated by the relative strength index, Chai says. Hence, futures could pull back toward the up-trending 20-day simple moving average, the analyst adds. Spot gold is 0.1% higher at $3,646.34/oz.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1934 ET - Pantoro Gold's stock has more than tripled in value since the start of the year, supported by prices of the precious metal repeatedly hitting new record highs. "Pantoro appears to have turned a corner operationally and we see upside to FY 2026 guidance given recent performance," analyst Ross Bennett says. "However, the recent stock-price rally has somewhat diminished short-term upside." Morgans starts Pantoro at "hold," with a A$5.33/share price target. It expects current commodity prices to help drive solid earnings and production growth for Pantoro. "These are complemented by >50% Ebitda margin in our base case," Morgans adds. Pantoro ended Thursday at A$5.35. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0907 ET - Aumovio's obligation to compensate BMW on its brake recall last year might be a stain on the company for some investors, UBS analysts write in a note. The Continental AG spinoff went public with a $4.1 billion valuation Thursday. Although UBS analysts cut the company's valuation by 700 million euros due to the fact that parent Continental supplied the faulty parts, the analysts consider compensation a manageable risk. The two companies could find a commercial agreement that softens the hit on Aumovio, they say. Aumovio shares are up 5.3% at 40.68 euros. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0753 ET - Tesla's pay package and capital expenditures plan for the coming years indicates it has ambitious targets for both product rollout and market cap, Baird analysts Ben Kallo and Davis Sunderland say, upgrading the stock to outperform from neutral. The Tesla board proposed a new set of product, financial and market cap milestones which are tied to a new pay package for Chief Executive Elon Musk, which the analysts see as a tool to incentivize and retain Musk for the next decade. Using those milestones, the analysts estimate shares will be $1,400 to $3,000 apiece by 2035. The analysts also see Musk's roughly $1 billion purchase of 2.6 million shares as a vote of confidence. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0739 ET - FedEx got some pushback from analysts about the feasibility of its expected 4% to 6% annual revenue growth guidance, given the anticipated $1 billion in additional costs from shifting trade policies. FedEx's revenue grew 3% in the F1Q, but Chief Customer Officer Brie Carere said that included a $280 million headwind from its partnership with the U.S. Postal Service ending. FedEx expects to get more revenue from its Amazon partnership later in the year, as it is still onboarding the e-commerce company, Carere said. Onboarding is expected to be complete by F3Q, which will boost U.S. domestic revenue growth. Fedex is up 4% premarket. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0655 ET - FedEx laid out revenue growth expectations ahead of Wall Street estimates, but Evercore ISI analyst Jonathan Chappell says uncertain trade policy will continue to limit expansion opportunities. Tariffs and the exemption of the de minimis loophole is now anticipated to hit earnings before interest and taxes by $850 million over the next three quarters, after a $150 million hit in the first quarter. The analysts see more downside risk than upside because of these expected costs, which they say will limit earnings per share growth and multiple expansion for at least the next six to nine months. Shares trade up 5% in pre-market trading. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0607 ET - China Railway Group is expected to see a recovery in growth for new orders for FY 2025, says OCBC's equity research team, pointing to the company's target of CNY2.8T for the period. OCBC says the issuance of special bonds in 2H25 and likely improved order execution are expected to support the recovery. The company also expects margins to improve through cost controls and lower financing costs. OCBC holds a buy rating on China Railway Group's stock, setting its fair value estimate at HK$5.39, with its H shares last closing at HK$3.76. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0543 ET - Kuehne + Nagel could face challenges in the second half, and Deutsche Bank forecasts a step down in profits in the third quarter, both on year and on quarter. "We see few short-term catalysts, with earnings momentum still likely to be under pressure, volatile tariff news flow, and the risk of a recession which we think is not baked into consensus forecasts," analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank's new 2025 EBIT forecast of 1.35 billion Swiss francs is now below the low-end of recently revised full-year guidance of 1.45 billion-1.65 billion francs. The bank downgrades the shipping and transport company's stock rating to hold from buy and lowers its target price to 182 francs from 225 francs. Shares fall 7.1% to 157.40 francs. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0530 ET - DHL Group's guidance looks stretched, Deutsche Bank analyst Andy Chu writes. This particularly in light of a worst case 200 million-euro hit from further changes in U.S. de minimis rules. The market backdrop is also unlikely to help DHL, with macroeconomic indicators in Europe and the U.S. flashing orange and red, in particular jobs data, Chu adds. Consumer confidence and spend is under pressure, while the implementation of tariffs and abolishment of de minimis rules in the U.S. have caused uncertainty, which is bad for freight companies, he says. Deutsche Bank downgrades DHL Group to hold from buy and lowers its target price to 42 euros from 47 euros. Shares fall 0.2% to 38.06 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0513 ET - Shares in container shipping companies are under pressure Friday as container freight rates on major routes out of Shanghai continue to fall. The Shanghai Containerized Freight Index has fallen over 14% in the last week, according to data published by the Shanghai Shipping Exchange. "Far East to U.S. west coast rates are now down 56% from its recent peak reached in week 23 and 61% down from week 1," analysts at JPMorgan said. Maersk shares are 5% lower, while Hapag-Lloyd shares trade 2.6% lower. Maritime research consultancy Drewry said in a note that its world container index--that tracks freight rates for 40-foot containers on eight major shipping routes--shows the major Transpacific and Asia-Europe routes are now aligned in a downwards trajectory. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0353 ET - Automakers likely face persistent volatility in the near term, but third-quarter sales started relatively well in Europe and the U.S., Berenberg analysts write. Strong product-launch momentum in the second half and into 2026 and 2027 should also provide some support amid continuing pricing pressure, they add. Headwinds from existing 27.5% U.S. tariffs on European vehicles will continue weighing on margins until the 15% tariffs come into effect. A further softening in China will pressure near-term estimates. Berenberg's top picks are BMW, Ferrari and Renault. BMW remains fundamentally strong, there is upside to Ferrari consensus earnings in 2026-27 and Renault valuation remains attractive, it adds. It upgrades Stellantis stock to buy from hold and lifts its price target to 9.50 euros from 9 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0206 ET - ComfortDelGro is expected to deliver near-term mid-teens net profit growth, resilient cash flows and a 6.3% dividend yield in 2026, says RHB analyst Shekhar Jaiswal. The bank highlights improving UK public transport margins, capacity for new mergers and acquisitions, and stable point-to-point revenue from taxis and private hires in Singapore as share price drivers. The Singapore-based transport operator has also pre-qualified for the contract to operate the Copenhagen Metro. While the short-term earnings impact may be modest, a successful bid would mark its strategic entry into continental Europe, enhance its profile for future global tenders and diversify earnings opportunities beyond Singapore, the UK, and Australia, Jaiswal adds. RHB maintains its buy rating on ComfortDelGro with a S$1.75 target price. Shares were recently at S$1.49. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

1927 ET [Dow Jones]--FedEx doesn't believe its sales growth in the fiscal first quarter were the result of consumers trying to pull ahead of tariffs, Chief Customer Officer Brie Carere says during a call with analysts. The shipping company did get a boost in July from Amazon Prime week, which helped the company log a 3% increase in total revenue for the quarter, Carere says. But American consumers appear resilient despite trade uncertainty, which Carere sees as an indicator that their demand for shipping will continue beyond the first quarter. "We saw strong volumes in July, but I don't necessarily see that as a pull forward," Carere says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Nasdaq Rises; Trump Announces TikTok Deal — WSJ
data.description:
By Caitlin McCabe

President Trump announced a TikTok deal with Chinese leader Xi Jinping after the two spoke today.

Trump and Xi didn't release details of the deal, but new investors would own about half the company, while existing ones in TikTok parent ByteDance including Susquehanna International, KKR and General Atlantic would hold roughly 30%.

In addition, lawmakers on Capitol Hill voted Friday to reject a short-term spending bill that aimed to avoid a government shutdown at the end of the month. The vote was 44 in favor and 48 against, short of the 60 required and sending GOP leaders back to the drawing board.

Stocks rose Friday, a day after the Russell 2000 and the three major U.S. stock indexes closed at all-time highs for the first time since November 2021.

The Bank of Japan kept interest rates on hold at the end of a week in which the Federal Reserve and several other central banks cut them. The BoJ said it would start offloading its portfolio of exchange-traded funds, a sign of confidence in the economy despite global trade tumult.

In Friday trading:

Treasury yields rose for a third day. The 10-year yield touched 4.14%.

Gold futures advanced above $3,680 a troy ounce, hovering near all-time highs.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Stephen Miran Makes His Case For More — WSJ
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

The newest Federal Reserve board member, Stephen Miran, argued for aggressive interest-rate cuts in his first television interview since taking the job, signaling he is likely to stay closely aligned with President Trump's demands for lower rates while serving at the central bank.

Miran said Friday morning on CNBC he doesn't believe that tariffs are causing inflation and that other federal policy changes are likely to ease the pace of price increases. He added he expects economic growth to improve in the second half of the year.

The Senate confirmed Miran to the Fed board late on Monday, just hours before Fed officials gathered to consider their interest-rate decision. On Wednesday, Miran was the only official to break with the committee's decision to lower rates by a quarter point, dissenting in favor of a larger half-point cut. He also laid out a faster path for projected future rate cuts than any of his colleagues did.

In an unusual arrangement, Miran is on unpaid leave from his job as chair of the White House Council of Economic Advisers while serving a truncated term at the Fed that ends in January. Miran would be able to stay at the Fed indefinitely, however, if Trump doesn't nominate a successor.

Democrats and some Fed watchers have criticized Miran's dual role at the White House and the central bank, arguing it is part of a larger assault on the Fed's traditional independence from presidential politics. Since returning to office, Trump has demanded sharp rate cuts and lambasted Fed Chair Jerome Powell for not cutting rates as quickly as Trump would like.

On Friday, Miran defended his dual role, saying that he will make decisions at the Fed based only on his own views.

"I will do independent analysis based on my interpretation of the data and based on my interpretation of the economy," Miran said. He added that he didn't tell Trump in advance how he would vote at this week's meeting.

Beyond his dissent on Wednesday, Miran also broke with other Fed officials by projecting a much sharper pace of rate cuts at the central bank's two remaining meetings this year. In the quarterly dot plot, Miran suggested that rates should fall below 3% in 2025, penciling in three more quarter-point cuts than any of his colleagues did.

Miran said Friday that his preference for more cuts stems from his skepticism that tariffs are causing inflation. He argued that because elevated price increases are likely to fade on their own, the Fed should ease policy quickly — down to a so-called neutral level — to avoid unnecessary damage to the labor market. The neutral rate of interest is the level that neither stimulates nor restrains growth, though economists disagree on what exactly that rate is at any given time.

"I don't see a reason for being so far from neutral at the moment," Miran said. "The longer you stay very restrictive, the greater the risks build up of significant misses to the employment mandate." The Fed is tasked with maintaining both low and stable inflation and sturdy labor markets.

Through July, the Fed held rates steady at elevated levels this year, partly out of concern that steep new tariffs could spark sustained price increases. Inflation is still hotter than the Fed's target, but a run of weaker job-market data has shifted Fed officials' attention toward their other responsibility, keeping unemployment in check.

"What's different now is you see a very different picture of the risks to the labor market," Powell said in a press conference Wednesday, after the Fed announced it would cut rates by a quarter point. Powell also signaled that Miran's preference for a steeper rate cut had been largely dismissed by other Fed officials. Many of them remain more concerned than Miran about elevated inflation.

"There wasn't widespread support at all for a 50-basis-point cut today," Powell said, using finance jargon for the half-percentage point Miran preferred.

In his Friday interview, Miran was quick to acknowledge that no other Fed officials shared his preference for such steep cuts, and said he hoped to persuade more of his colleagues to his perspective over time. Miran, 42, is a Harvard-trained economist who worked on Wall Street before catching the attention of the president's inner circle during Trump's 2024 campaign by writing up detailed economic arguments backing Trump's agenda in a series of papers.

In his first days at the Fed, Miran looks likely to keep up an active public-speaking schedule on the job. He is expected to appear on another cable-news show Friday afternoon and plans to speak to a club of economists in New York City on Monday.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

data.title: Idacorp Raises Quarterly Dividend by 2%
data.description:
By Dean Seal

The board of Idacorp has raised the company's quarterly cash dividend by 2%, to 88 cents a share.

The new payout, equal to $3.52 a year, represents an annual yield of about 2.8% based on Friday's closing price of $125.70.

The Boise, Idaho, holding company for utility Idaho Power has increased its dividend every year since 2011, resulting in a compound annual dividend growth of 7.4% since that time and a cumulative increase of 193%, Chief Executive Lisa Grow said.

data.title: Wall St closes higher with indexes notching weekly gains
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes up: Dow 0.37%, S&P 500 0.49%, Nasdaq 0.72%
  • FedEx results top targets on cost-cutting, shares jump
  • Lennar drops on weak forecast
  • Trump, Xi make trade progress in first call since June

By Abigail Summerville and Purvi Agarwal

Wall Street's main indexes closed higher on Friday and also posted a weekly gain, as FedEx rose after positive earnings.

The parcel delivery company popped 2.1% after it reported quarterly profit and revenue above analyst estimates on Thursday, as cost-cutting and strength in domestic deliveries helped offset weaker international volumes.

Apple rose 3.2% following a price target raise from J.P. Morgan, while gains in Palantir Technologies and Oracle also drove the S&P 500 technology sector up 1.19%.

Seven of the 11 S&P sector indexes rose, while energy stocks were the biggest drag.

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq notched their third straight week of gains, boosted by the Fed's first rate cut of 2025 and indications of further monetary policy easing. Revived optimism around AI-linked stock trading also added to the rise.

Wall Street wavered earlier in the day as investors continued to digest the Fed's outlook and get a read on Stephen Miran, its newest governor and White House economic adviser, who spoke on CNBC on Friday morning.

"Certainly if the idea is the Fed is moving in a direction to relax the inflation target, that is definitely a recipe for running hot, and that's good for stocks," said Scott Ladner, chief investment officer at Horizon Investments.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 172.85 points, or 0.37%, to 46,315.27, the S&P 500 gained 32.40 points, or 0.49%, to 6,664.36 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 160.75 points, or 0.72%, to 22,631.48.

For the week, the S&P 500 rose 1.2%, the Nasdaq climbed 2.2% and the Dow added 1.05%.

The small-cap Russell 2000 index dropped 0.71% after briefly hitting an intraday record high. It notched a record close on Thursday, its first since November 2021.

"Small caps have been trading inversely with rates, and it's just the idea of small caps benefiting disproportionately from lower interest rates," Ladner said.

Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping spoke by phone, after which Trump said that the two leaders made progress on a TikTok deal and agreed to a face-to-face meeting as soon as next month in South Korea.

Also on Friday, the Senate blocked a short-term funding bill, increasing the likelihood of a U.S. government shutdown.

Wall Street's three main indexes are in positive territory so far in September - a month traditionally deemed bad for U.S. equities. The benchmark S&P 500 has shed 1.4% on average in the month since 2000, according to data compiled by LSEG.

In other stock news, Lennar fell 4.2% after the homebuilder reported a lower third-quarter profit and forecast fourth-quarter home deliveries below estimates.

Paramount Skydance jumped 5.9% after a CNBC report laid out more details about the media company's potential bid for Warner Bros Discovery , which rose 3.4%. An offer could come later than previously expected, according to CNBC.

Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.43-to-1 ratio on the NYSE, and by a 1.42-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted 30 new 52-week highs and 17 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 151 new highs and 54 new lows.

Volume on U.S. exchanges was 26.33 billion shares, compared with the 17.41 billion average for the full session over the last 20 trading days.

data.title: Head on the Street Recap: TikTok Is Saved Again — WSJ
data.description:
By Heard Editors

What Happened in Markets Today

TikTok deal here - but details are scant. President Trump said he and Chinese leader Xi Jinping reached an agreement for a group of investors to take control of TikTok's U.S. operations. Much remains unknown--including how the new U.S.-based app will work under the framework of the deal.

Social media stocks slide. A TikTok deal was widely expected, but still sparked some weakness for U.S.-based rivals. Instagram owner Meta Platforms saw its shares slip a fraction on Friday, while Snapchat-parent Snap dropped 3.3%. Oracle--the software giant leading the consortium taking control of U.S. TikTok--jumped more than 4%, building on AI-driven gains that already propelled the stock up 78% so far this year.

Disney's Jimmy Kimmel decision reverberates. Disney, which owns the ABC network that suspended the late-night host, saw its shares slip 1% Friday - a notable standout against gains by most other major media stocks.

Stocks close week on a high note. Major U.S. indices rose Friday and locked in gains for the week. The Nasdaq closed its second consecutuve week with a 2% gain. while the S&P 500 rose 1.2% for the week.

FedEx delivers. Shipping giant's stock picks up 2.3% despite the company warning of a $1 billion hit to full-year earnings due to tariffs. Revenue still beat Wall Street's projections in the latest fiscal quarter.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: TSX adds to weekly winning streak as gold mining shares jump
data.description:
Key points:
  • TSX ends up 1.1% at 29,768.36
  • Surpasses Thursday's record closing high
  • Materials sector jumps 3.9% as gold price climbs
  • Barrick Mining climbs 9.7%

By Fergal Smith

Canada's main stock index rose to another record high on Friday as gold mining shares climbed and investors cheered the Bank of Canada's recent move to support the domestic economy.

The S&P/TSX composite index ended up 314.83 points, or 1.1%, at 29,768.36, surpassing Thursday's record closing high.

For the week, the index was up 1.7%, its seventh straight weekly gain. That is the longest such streak since February 2024.

"The Bank of Canada's recent rate cut was a shot in the arm that the market was looking for," said Victor Kuntzevitsky, a portfolio manager at Stonehaven, Wellington-Altus Private Counsel.

"It's a clear signal that the bank is confident in the inflation trajectory and is now pivoting to support economic expansion. And in our view, this has fundamentally repriced risk for investors and provided a clear tailwind for Canadian equities."

Canadian retail sales fell 0.8% in July but an advanced indicator pointed to sales rebounding by 1% in August. On Wednesday, the BoC reduced its key policy rate to a three-year low of 2.5% and said it was prepared to cut further if risks to the economy rose.

The materials sector , which includes fertilizer companies and metal mining shares, jumped 3.9%, as the price of gold moved closer to another record high.

Gold and copper producer Barrick Mining Corporation was the top-performing stock, with its shares climbing 9.7%.

"The surge in gold stocks reflects a disciplined focus on cost management among Canadian producers," Kuntzevitsky said. "We really feel that investors are rewarding companies with strong operational efficiencies that can turn higher gold prices directly into shareholder value."

The consumer staples sector (.GSPTTCS) gained 1.3% and heavily weighted financials (.SPTTFS) were up 0.9%.

Just two of 10 major sectors ended lower, including energy . Energy lost 2.2% as the price of oil settled 1.4% lower at $62.68 a barrel on worries about large supplies and declining demand.
